coredata.dc:title: A photo-bio-hybrid catalyst with a triple synergistic enhancement effect to boost oxygen reduction for high-performance biofuel cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Dandan
Chen, Haoran
Su, Qiwen
Zhang, Jian-Rong
Wang, Linlin
Zhu, Jun-Jie
coredata.dc:description: Bilirubin oxidase (BOD)-catalyzed oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) exhibits outstanding advantages compared to abiotic electro-catalysts in neutral environment, thus playing a crucial role in fuel cells. However, limited by the low solubility of oxygen and the impeded extracellular electron transfer, the activity of BOD is fast-approaching its upper threshold. Given this, we create a mutually enhanced bio-abiotic hybrid ORR photo-electro-catalyst by integrating BOD with pyrrole nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (BOD/Npyo-CDs). In BOD/Npyo-CDs, Npyo-CDs generate photoelectrons and vacancies under illumination, and the vacancies and BOD simultaneously accept electrons from the anode. The photoelectrons can rapidly transfer to BOD's active center due to the high electron conductivity of Npyo-CDs and well-matched energy level, further boosting the ORR on BOD. This process promotes photoelectrons-vacancies separation of Npyo-CDs and reduces fluorescence energy dissipation, which in turn promotes the ORR by generating more photoelectrons. Thanks to the triple synergistic promotion effect, the current density of BOD/Npyo-CDs reaches 2.76 mA cm−2. Coupled with an Au nanozyme anode, we develop a glucose/oxygen fuel cell with remarkable power output (468 μW cm−2) and high stable open-circuit voltage (0.78 V, 70 h). This study shed light on breaking the limitation in energy conversion efficiency by creating a mutually promoted bio-abiotic catalyst.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bio-abiotic hybrid catalyst
Carbon nanodots
Mutual promotion effect
Enzymatic biofuel cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design, synthesis, and characterizations of N-CDs
Construction and performance of BOD/Npyo-CDs photo-bio-hybrid cathode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic representation of a glucose/O2 biofuel cell incorporating a photo-bio-hybrid cathodic catalyst (BOD/Npyo-CDs) exhibits a triple synergistic enhancement effect to enhance oxygen reduction.

Morphology and composition of the carbon nanodots. Corresponding transmission electron microscope (TEM) images and size distributions of (A) CDs, (B) Npyo-CDs, and (C) Npyd-CDs synthesized using succinic acid, aspartic acid and asparagine as precursors, respectively. High-resolution N1s XPS patterns and corresponding N-species (pyridinic N: 398.0–399.0 eV, pyrrolic N: 399.5–400.5 eV) of (D) Npyo-CDs and (E) Npyd-CDs.

Optical and electrical properties of carbon nanodots. (A) UV–Vis spectroscopy, (B) calculated band gap value, (C) schematic illustration of the band gap structures, (D) time-resolved fluorescence decay kinetics curves, (E) photocurrent density responses, and (F) EISs of CDs, Npyo-CDs, and Npyd-CDs.

(A) The CLSM images of BOD/Npyo-CDs; BOD was labeled with RhB, which shows red fluorescence at an excitation of 543 nm, and Npyo-CDs shows blue fluorescence at an excitation of 380 nm. Scale bars = 20 μm. (B) LSVs curves of Npyo-CDs, BOD, and BOD/Npyo-CDs in PB (0.2 M, pH 7.4) at N2 or O2 saturation at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. (C) Impedance Nyquist plot of the Npyo-CDs, BOD, and BOD/Npyo-CDs. (D) Photocurrent density response of Npyo-CDs, BOD, and BOD/Npyo-CDs in PB (0.2 M, pH 7.4) saturated O2 (0.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl). (E) Time-resolved fluorescence decay kinetics curves of Npyo-CDs and BOD/Npyo-CDs (excitation: 365 nm; emission: 420 nm). (F) The CLSM images of Npyo-CDs and BOD/Npyo-CDs and corresponding fluorescence intensity. The excitation wavelength was set at 380 nm, scale bars = 20 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Polarization curves and power output under 200 mM glucose concentration at a scan rate of 1 mV s−1. (B) Open-circuit voltage stability of EBFCs with Npyo-CDs, BOD, and BOD/Npyo-CDs cathodes, coupled with AuNPs anodes, under illumination. (C) Performance comparison of biofuel cells using BOD as the cathode. (D) Discharge stability at 0.3 V for EBFCs with Npyo-CDs, BOD, and BOD/Npyo-CDs cathodes, coupled with AuNPs anodes, under illumination.

head.ce:title: A photo-bio-hybrid catalyst with a triple synergistic enhancement effect to boost oxygen reduction for high-performance biofuel cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University, Nanjing, 210023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210023, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Chemical Additives for Industry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi'an, 710021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Chemical Additives for Industry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Shaanxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Chemical Additives for Industry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi'an 710021, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bilirubin oxidase (BOD)-catalyzed oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) exhibits outstanding advantages compared to abiotic electro-catalysts in neutral environment, thus playing a crucial role in fuel cells. However, limited by the low solubility of oxygen and the impeded extracellular electron transfer, the activity of BOD is fast-approaching its upper threshold. Given this, we create a mutually enhanced bio-abiotic hybrid ORR photo-electro-catalyst by integrating BOD with pyrrole nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (BOD/Npyo-CDs). In BOD/Npyo-CDs, Npyo-CDs generate photoelectrons and vacancies under illumination, and the vacancies and BOD simultaneously accept electrons from the anode. The photoelectrons can rapidly transfer to BOD's active center due to the high electron conductivity of Npyo-CDs and well-matched energy level, further boosting the ORR on BOD. This process promotes photoelectrons-vacancies separation of Npyo-CDs and reduces fluorescence energy dissipation, which in turn promotes the ORR by generating more photoelectrons. Thanks to the triple synergistic promotion effect, the current density of BOD/Npyo-CDs reaches 2.76 mA cm−2. Coupled with an Au nanozyme anode, we develop a glucose/oxygen fuel cell with remarkable power output (468 μW cm−2) and high stable open-circuit voltage (0.78 V, 70 h). This study shed light on breaking the limitation in energy conversion efficiency by creating a mutually promoted bio-abiotic catalyst.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Enzymatic biofuel cells (EBFCs) are a novel class of eco-friendly and bio-friendly energy conversion systems that convert chemical energy stored in biological molecules into electrical energy via redox enzymes [1–4]. The cathode of EBFCs generally undergoes the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), making the ORR performance of the cathode a crucial determinant of the overall EBFC performance [5]. In this eco-friendly and bio-friendly electro-catalytic system, biological enzymes such as bilirubin oxidase (BOD) and laccase exhibit superior catalytic ORR performance, surpassing noble-metal catalysts [6,7]. However, the ORR current density at the cathode is restricted to less than 2 mA cm−2, despite considerable efforts to improve performance. These strategies include the utilization of nanomaterials (e.g., carbon nanotubes/nanodots, gold clusters, and polymers) [8–10], the application of directional immobilization strategies for facilitating direct electron transfer [11], and the incorporation of polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) submicron rod materials or microalgae to enhance the local oxygen concentration [12,13]. The primary challenge stems from low oxygen solubility, which leads to a slow mass transfer rate, and the hindered electron transfer from natural enzymes with electrodes results in a sluggish ORR rate. Thus, the ORR performance catalyzed by the enzyme is fast approaching the upper threshold. This demonstrates that further improvement of EBFC performance through a single chemical-to-electrical energy conversion pathway is difficult [14]. Solar energy is an inexhaustible green energy source from nature and exhibits excellent environmental and biological friendliness [15]. Therefore, establishing a coupled chemical-energy and solar-energy conversion pathway would represent a breakthrough in enhancing ORR at the cathode, thereby further improving EBFC performance.

The key to establishing an efficient photo-enhanced EBFC is fabricating photoelectric materials with high photoelectric conversion efficiency and excellent biocompatibility to avoid impairing the enzyme's activity while introducing solar energy. Compared to conventional inorganic photocatalysts, carbon nanodots (CDs) possess merits of outstanding biocompatibility, cost-effectiveness, and environmental sustainability. Therefore, the CDs show significant advantages in coupling with biological enzymes as they hardly harm the enzyme. What's more, the size of the CDs (∼5 nm) is well matched with that of the enzyme, which is highly helpful in accelerating the extra molecular electron transfer rate [16]. Our research group has integrated photo-responsive nitrogen-sulfur-doped carbon nanodots with Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 to develop a photo-assisted microbial anode, resulting in a 2.6-fold increase in output power for microbial fuel cells [17]. Despite the progress made in the application of CDs within photoelectrochemical fields [18–21], the efficient integration of CDs with natural enzymes to develop a highly effective photo-enhanced EBFC remains a formidable challenge. The first major challenge lies in CDs' limited photoelectric conversion efficiency, primarily due to the dissipation of photogenerated electrons as fluorescence [22]. Another significant challenge is that photogenerated electrons and holes are easily trapped in defects and on the surfaces of CDs, severely hindering charge mobility and separation and ultimately limiting photoelectric conversion performance [23].

Despite great challenges, the redox enzymes in EBFC hint at new solutions. The enzymes that catalyze the ORR in EBFCs, such as BOD and laccase, possess redox-active sites with high redox potentials (∼0.8–1.1 V vs. RHE) [24]. Therefore, BOD or laccase can theoretically accept electrons with lower potentials than the enzyme's active sites [25]. Thus, if we can fabricate CDs with the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy level lower than the redox potentials of the enzyme's (BOD or laccase) active center, then the photogenerated electrons from CDs could transfer to the enzyme's active sites. As a result, energy loss in photoelectrons is expected to be reduced, thereby improving the photon-to-electric energy conversion efficiency. At this cathode, besides the electrons from the anode, the photogenerated electrons by CDs can also be utilized by BOD or laccase to catalyze the ORR, facilitating the ORR further. It is also noteworthy that the particle size of CDs can be controlled to be less than 5 nm, which is smaller than the enzyme size [6]. Therefore, CDs can approach the active centers of BOD or laccase. It is highly promising that the electron transfer rate between CDs and BOD/laccase is rapid. Eventually, the chemical-energy to electric-energy conversion efficiency can be further enhanced. Together with ingenious design, integrating enzymes and CDs will make a great breakthrough in boosting ORR for promoting EBFC performance. To the best of our knowledge, however, this strategy has not been reported to date.

In this study, a bio-abiotic hybrid ORR catalyst with triple synergistic enhancement effects is developed, utilizing BOD as the biological catalyst and well-designed pyrrole nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (Npyo-CDs) as the abiotic photoelectrocatalyst. Strategically, through doping nitrogen elements and regulating the nitrogen species, the Npyo-CD's LUMO energy level and band gap are reduced to −0.19 V vs. NHE and 3.01 eV, respectively. The Npyo-CDs are tightly coupled with BOD, whose active center has high redox potential. Mechanistically, the narrow band gap enables the Npyo-CDs to absorb more photons. Significantly, the LUMO energy level of Npyo-CDs is considerably lower than the redox potential of BOD (∼0.8–1.1 V vs. RHE, ∼0.4–0.7 V vs. NHE); thus, the Npyo-CDs generated photo-electrons can transfer to the active center of BOD, and their energy loss can be inhibited. In addition, the electrons from the anode can fill the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the Npyo-CDs, further promoting the photogenerated electron-hole separation of the Npyo-CDs, which ultimately enhances their photoelectrocatalytic performance. In this context, BOD can simultaneously utilize electrons from both the anode and the photoelectrons generated by Npyo-CDs to enhance cathode ORR. Furthermore, the Npyo-CDs have excellent electron conductivity. Therefore, the electron transfer rate between the electrode, the Npyo-CDs, and the BOD can be significantly boosted. Consequently, under illumination, the ORR current at the BOD/Npyo-CDs cathode increases 1.87 times compared to BOD. And coupling the BOD/Npyo-CDs cathode with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) anode, we develop a glucose/O2 biofuel cell with a high power output of approximately 468 μW cm−2 and a high stability of up to 70 h (Fig. 1 ). This study renovates the combined use of biological electrocatalysts and abiotic photocatalysts and is significant for developing an integrated energy conversion system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design, synthesis, and characterizations of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate and regulate the structure-activity relationship of CDs, we synthesized three kinds of CDs: the CDs without heteroelement doping (CDs), the pyrrole nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (Npyo-CDs), and the pyridine nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (Npyd-CDs) (Fig. S1). Firstly, the morphology and composition of the CDs, Npyo-CDs, and Npyd-CDs are explored. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Fig. 2 A–C) reveal that the CDs, Npyo-CDs, and Npyd-CDs exhibit uniform particle sizes with average diameters of 1.95 nm, 2.29 nm, and 2.58 nm, respectively (insets of Fig. 2A–C). The ultra-small size of CDs, Npyo-CDs, and Npyd-CDs facilitate proximity to the active center of BOD, significantly reducing the electron tunneling distance between BOD, CDs/Npyo-CDs/Npyd-CDs, and the electrode. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) analysis revealed (Fig. S2) that the CDs, Npyo-CDs, and Npyd-CDs contain -COOH group, due to observations of O-H (3510 cm−1), C=O (1726 cm−1), and C-O (1240 cm−1) vibrations. In contrast, Npyo-CDs and Npyd-CDs also display characteristic peaks for N-H stretching vibrations (3449 cm−1) and C-N bending vibrations (1400 cm−1), confirming nitrogen-doped success. From the FT-IR results, two key conclusions can be drawn: The CDs, Npyo-CDs, and Npyd-CDs possessed abundant active chemical groups, which makes them easy to bind to BOD through strong chemical bonds, and nitrogen is successfully doped into Npyo-CDs and Npyd-CDs.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) test is conducted to further determine the specific elemental types. The XPS results confirm the successful doping of nitrogen elements in Npyo-CDs and Npyd-CDs again (Fig. 2D–E and S3). Particularly, the high-resolution N1s spectra demonstrate that using aspartic acid or asparagine as precursors enables the different nitrogen species to be doped in carbon nanodots (Fig. 2D and E). Specifically, for the Npyo-CDs synthesized via the pyrolysis of aspartic acid, pyrrolic N (400.2 eV) is identified as the dominant nitrogen species, accounting for 88.93 % of the total nitrogen (Fig. 2D). For the Npyd-CDs synthesized via the pyrolysis of asparagine, pyridinic N (399.0 eV) is the predominant nitrogen species, accounting for 82.09 % of the total nitrogen (Fig. 2E). It has been reported that pyrrolic N and pyridinic N exhibit distinct functional roles in modulating the electronic configuration of catalysts [26]. Therefore, it is deduced that the Npyo-CDs and Npyd-CDs likely possess different photo-electrochemical properties.

The X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) test primarily supports the conclusion above. Specifically, the diffraction peaks of CDs, Npyo-CDs, and Npyd-CDs are observed at different diffraction angles of 27°, 26°, and 21°, respectively. The diffraction peak of Npyo-CDs is sharper than those of CDs and Npyd-CDs (Fig. S4), suggesting that the Npyo-CDs exhibit a higher degree of crystallinity, thus reducing grain boundaries and defects. This higher crystallinity effectively minimizes the trapping of photogenerated electrons by defects and surface grain boundaries, thereby enhancing charge mobility and separation [27]. Consequently, the photocurrent of Npyo-CDs is anticipated to increase.

UV–Vis spectroscopy was performed to understand further the optical properties of CDs, Npyo-CDs, and Npyd-CDs. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the CDs sample displays a distinct absorption peak at 260 nm, corresponding to the π-π∗ transition of C=C bonds. In contrast, the absorption peaks of Npyo-CDs and Npyd-CDs are observed at 290 nm. This UV–Vis absorption peak is mainly from the n-π∗ transition of C=O groups. Additionally, compared to CDs, a red shift in the absorption edges of Npyo-CDs and Npyd-CDs is observed, along with an increase in absorption intensity. This indicates that nitrogen doping can enhance the light absorption capability of carbon nanodots, thereby increasing the charge carrier density, which is favorable for enhancing photocurrent. To estimate the band gap energies of CDs, Npyo-CDs, and Npyd-CDs, the UV–Vis data were analyzed using the Kubelka-Munk function, with linear extrapolation applied to the Tauc plot [28]. As shown in Fig. 3B, the band gaps of CDs, Npyo-CDs, and Npyd-CDs were determined to be 3.18, 3.01, and 3.11 eV, respectively. Clearly, Npyo-CDs exhibit a narrower bandgap compared to CDs and Npyd-CDs, resulting in more photogenerated carrier production.

As previously mentioned, the energy levels of the LUMO and HOMO determine the ability of the photogenerated electrons to transfer to the enzyme and the ability of the photogenerated holes to accept electrons, respectively. Given this, linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was performed to investigate the energy levels of the LUMO and HOMO in CDs, Npyo-CDs, and Npyd-CDs [29,30]. The reduction potentials of CDs, Npyo-CDs, and Npyd-CDs are measured to be −0.35, −0.39, and −0.37 V vs. Ag/AgCl (−0.15, −0.19, and −0.17 V vs. NHE), respectively. Correspondingly, the LUMO energy levels are determined to be −7.49, −7.28, and −7.40 eV, respectively (Fig. S5, See section 1.5 of the Supporting Information for the exact calculations process). Notably, the reduction potentials of Npyo-CDs are the lowest and significantly lower than BOD's redox potential (∼0.4–0.7 V vs. NHE). This suggests that photoelectrons generated by Npyo-CDs are more efficiently transferred to the active center of BOD, facilitating charge carrier separation within the Npyo-CDs and minimizing the energy loss of photoelectrons. Combining the band gap values from Fig. 3B, the HOMO energy levels of CDs, Npyo-CDs, and Npyd-CDs are estimated to be −4.31, −4.27, and −4.29 eV, respectively. This finding suggests that three kinds of carbon dots are capable of accepting the electrons from the anode of EBFC (Fig. 3C). Overall, Npyo-CDs exhibit lower LUMO energy levels and a narrower band gap than CDs and Npyd-CDs. This indicates that nitrogen doping enhances the photoelectric activity of carbon nanodots, with pyrrolic N being particularly favorable for constructing synergistically enhanced bio-abiotic hybrid ORR catalysts.

Considering that fluorescence lifetimes serve as a reliable indicator to evaluate the kinetics of photogenerated carrier separation, we also analyzed the time-resolved fluorescence decay kinetics curves for three types of carbon nanodots. (Fig. 3D). The results revealed that the fluorescence lifetimes of CDs are 5.46 ns. Notably, the lifetimes of Npyo-CDs and Npyd-CDs significantly increase to 10.6 ns and 9.10 ns, respectively. These findings demonstrate that nitrogen doping effectively suppresses interlayer energy dissipation, and pyrrole N has a better-suppressing effect than pyridine N. The suppression of energy dissipation primarily arises from two factors. First, as revealed by XRD, the diffraction peaks of Npyo-CDs and Npyd-CDs exhibit negative shifts compared to CDs, indicating an increase in interlayer spacing following nitrogen doping [27]. The increased interlayer spacing can reduce the π-π stacking, eventually suppressing non-radiative energy dissipation between layers [22]. Second, the nitrogen element is an excellent electron donor, effectively scavenging photogenerated holes to inhibit electron-hole recombination, thereby prolonging the lifetimes of photoelectrons [23]. All of the above indicates that pyrrole N is more effective in enhancing the photoelectric performance of CDs.

To further explore the photoelectrochemical properties of three kinds of carbon nanodots, transient photocurrent density and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed. As shown in Fig. 3E, at 0.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl, the photocurrent density of CDs is as low as 9 μA cm−2. In contrast, the photocurrent densities of Npyd-CDs and Npyo-CDs increase significantly to 14 μA cm−2 and 25 μA cm−2, respectively. The enhanced photocurrent density indicates that Npyo-CDs exhibit the highest electron-hole separation efficiency and more available charge carriers for subsequent BOD-catalyzed ORR. Furthermore, the EIS results reveal that the charge transfer impedances of CDs, Npyo-CDs, and Npyd-CDs are 63 Ω, 30 Ω, and 38 Ω, respectively (Fig. 3F). This indicates that nitrogen doping can reduce charge transfer impedance, which enhances the electron transfer rate between the electrode and BOD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and performance of BOD/Npyo-CDs photo-bio-hybrid cathode
ce:section.ce:para: Through investigating and regulating the effects of the nitrogen species on the photoelectrochemical properties of the CDs, we have obtained the Npyo-CDs, which show distinctive advantages to combining with BOD for construct a mutually enhanced bio-abiotic hybrid photoelectrochemical catalyst (Fig. S6). Subsequently, a BOD/Npyo-CDs hybrid cathodic catalyst is designed using the 1-pyrenebutyric acid N-hydroxysuccinimide ester (PBSE) as a linker to facilitate the close binding of Npyo-CDs to BOD [31,32].

Firstly, confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) and FT-IR spectroscopy tests were conducted to monitor the fabrication of BOD/Npyo-CDs. As shown in Fig. 4 A, pure Npyo-CDs and the RhB-labeled BOD emit blue and red fluorescence, respectively. After linking by PBSE, intense pink fluorescence was observed, resulting from the superimposed fluorescent color of blue and red fluorescence. In addition, the new infrared absorption peaks at 1650 cm−1 and 1384 cm−1, corresponding to the C=O and C-N vibrational modes of the amide group, respectively, were also observed (Fig. S7). These results confirm the successful fabrication of the BOD/Npyo-CDs.

To optimize the ratio of BOD and Npyo-CDs, the photoelectrochemical performance of BOD/Npyo-CDs, synthesized with varying Npyo-CDs concentrations, was evaluated by measuring the catalytic current densities (at 0.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl) under O2-saturated conditions. Results in Fig. S8 revealed that the catalytic current density increased rapidly with the increasing concentration of Npyo-CDs, ranging from 0.5 mg mL−1 to 3.0 mg mL−1. However, the current density decreases slightly as the Npyo-CDs increase further. This can be attributed to the aggregation of Npyo-CDs at higher concentrations, which reduces the active surface area and impedes electron transport. Moreover, aggregation hinders light penetration, further diminishing the overall photoelectrocatalytic performance. Given this, the BOD/Npyo-CDs with the optimal photoelectrochemical performance were fabricated using 3 mg mL−1 Npyo-CDs.

Then, a comparative study of the photoelectrochemical performance of Npyo-CDs, BOD, and the BOD/Npyo-CDs cathodes was examined using the LSV test. As shown in Fig. 4B, for the Npyo-CDs and BOD under dark conditions, the ORR current density (at 0 V vs. Ag/AgCl) is only −0.81 mA cm−2 and -1.22 mA cm−2, respectively. Obviously, the ORR current density (at 0 V vs. Ag/AgCl) increased to 0.87 mA cm−2 and 1.48 mA cm−2 under illumination conditions, respectively. Introducing solar energy resulted in a 7.4 % and 21 % ORR current increase for the Npyo-CDs and BOD cathodes, respectively. Notably, for the BOD/Npyo-CDs cathode, under dark conditions, the ORR current density is 2.10 mA cm−2 (at 0 V vs. Ag/AgCl), exceeding those of the Npyo-CDs and BOD cathodes. Moreover, under illumination, the ORR current density of the BOD/Npyo-CDs cathode exhibits a 32 % increase, reaching up to 2.76 mA cm-2 (at 0 V vs. Ag/AgCl). These results powerfully demonstrate the synergistic effect between Npyo-CDs and BOD in boosting the photo-assisted ORR performance of the BOD/Npyo-CDs. Moreover, BOD/Npyo-CDs exhibited increased incident photon-to-current efficiency (IPCE) compared to pure BOD, indicating the crucial role of Npyo-CDs in enhancing light-to-electron conversion (Fig. S9). The Tafel slopes derived from the LSV curves revealed that the slope for BOD/Npyo-CDs (106 mV·dec−1) is significantly lower than that of pure BOD cathode (302 mV·dec−1), suggesting that the incorporation of Npyo-CDs significantly enhances the ORR kinetics catalyzed by BOD, as shown in Fig. S10. In addition, compared to the BOD cathode, the onset potential of ORR at the BOD/Npyo-CDs cathode shifted from 0.54 V to 0.46 V. Moreover, ORR tests were performed on a rotating ring disk electrode (RRDE) to calculate the apparent electron transfer number. Based on the LSV curves in Fig. S11, the apparent electron transfer numbers of the bare BOD, BOD/Npyo-CDs were calculated to be 4.00 and 4.01, respectively. This result indicates that the ORR catalyzed by the BOD and BOD/Npyo-CDs proceeded via the fast four-electron reduction pathway, which can significantly enhance the energy conversion efficiency by completely reduce the O2 into H2O. And as the EIS results revealed, the electron resistance of BOD/Npyo-CDs cathode (42 Ω) is significantly lower than that of BOD cathode (65 Ω), as depicted in Fig. 4C. These results suggest that Npyo-CDs facilitate electron transfer between BOD and the electrode, thereby reducing the overpotential for ORR. Considering that the catalytic current density at the BOD/Npyo-CDs cathode exceeds the sum of the current density at pure BOD and Npyo-CDs cathodes, indicating that the enhanced performance at the BOD/Npyo-CDs cathode is not merely a superposition of the Npyo-CDs generated photocurrent and the BOD catalyzed ORR current. This demonstrates a mutual enhancement between BOD and Npyo-CDs.

To further validate this, the photo-responsiveness of the BOD/Npyo-CDs cathodes was evaluated through transient photocurrent density measurements. Under illumination, the ORR current densities at the BOD/CDs and BOD/Npyd-CDs cathodes were −1.38 mA cm−2 and -2.29 mA cm−2, respectively, both lower than that at the BOD/Npyo-CDs cathode. This demonstrates that Npyo-CDs enhance the BOD-catalyzed ORR performance by generating more photoelectrons (Fig. S12), which agrees with the analysis in Fig. 3. In addition, as illustrated in Fig. 4D, the photocurrent density of the BOD/Npyo-CDs cathode reached −211 μA cm−2, significantly higher than that of the pure Npyo-CDs cathode (−25 μA cm−2) and pure BOD cathode (−58 μA cm−2). This result further suggests that BOD can enhance the photocurrent density of BOD/Npyo-CDs by receiving photoelectrons generated from Npyo-CDs.

As we have proved above, the LUMO energy level of Npyo-CDs (−0.19 V vs. NHE, Fig. 3C) is lower than T1 Cu redox potential of BOD (0.46 V vs. Ag/AgCl from Fig. 4B, equivalent, 0.66 V vs. NHE) as shown in Fig. S13. This confirms that BOD can accept photoelectrons generated by Npyo-CDs to catalyze ORR. Fluorescence correlation tests further confirmed the photogenerated electron transfer between Npyo-CDs and BOD. Initially, the fluorescence lifetime of Npyo-CDs is 10.6 ns. However, after complexation with BOD, the fluorescence lifetime decreases to 6.05 ns (Fig. 4E). Consistent with this, CLSM results show a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity of Npyo-CDs after complexation with BOD, indicating that BOD could burst the fluorescence of Npyo-CDs (Fig. 4F). Fig. 4E and F provide direct evidence of photogenerated electron transfer between Npyo-CDs and BOD, suggesting that BOD can inhibit fluorescence-induced photoelectron energy dissipation by receiving and utilizing the photogenerated electrons from Npyo-CDs, thus further improving the photoelectric conversion efficiency of Npyo-CDs. In summary, using the BOD/Npyo-CDs cathode is expected to construct high-performance EBFC.

Au nanoparticles (AuNPs), which have high activity for glucose oxidation, were employed as the anode, meanwhile BOD/Npyo-CDs served as the cathode to construct a glucose-oxygen biofuel cell. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) analysis confirms the successful decoration of AuNPs on the carbon paper electrode by electrodeposition, and XRD results suggest that the AuNPs predominantly displayed the (111) crystalline surface (Fig. S14), which is renowned for its superior catalytic activity toward glucose oxidation [33]. As LSV depicted, a direct correlation between glucose concentration and oxidation current, with a maximum peak current of 6.03 mA achieved at 0.34 V under a glucose concentration of 200 mM (Fig. S15). Three-electrode LSV scans demonstrated that the anode generated a significantly higher current (2.65 mA) than the BOD/Npyo-CDs cathode (1.16 mA, absolute value) at 0.0 V (vs. Ag/AgCl). These results suggest that the overall performance of the EBFC is primarily determined by the BOD/Npyo-CDs cathode (Fig. S16).

In neutral electrolytes containing 200 mM glucose and under illumination, the BOD/Npyo-CDs-catalyzed glucose/O2 EBFC achieves a remarkable voltage of 0.78 V and a power density of 468 μW cm−2 (Fig. 5 A). In contrast, the glucose/O2 EBFCs catalyzed pure by BOD or Npyo-CDs exhibit open-circuit voltages of 0.72 V and 0.66 V, respectively. Notably, their maximum power densities are limited to 231 μW cm−2 and 70 μW cm−2, respectively (Fig. S17). Under dark conditions, however, the maximum power density outputs of the glucose/O2 EBFCs catalyzed by BOD, Npyo-CDs, and BOD/Npyo-CDs decreased to 187 μW cm−2, 46 μW cm−2, and 235 μW cm−2, respectively (Fig. S18). Three key conclusions can be derived from the results: First, Npyo-CDs enhance the bio-catalytic activity of BOD, improving EBFC performance by facilitating electron transfer between BOD and the electrode due to their exceptional electron conductivity and ultra-small size. Second, Npyo-CDs absorb light energy to generate photogenerated charges, supplying additional electrons to BOD for enhanced ORR, thus achieving dual improvement in EBFC performance. Finally, Npyo-CDs produce vacancies that can receive electrons from the anode, thus enhancing the ability of the cathode to receive electrons and enabling a third enhancement in performance. Collectively, this triple enhancement enables the performance of the EBFC to exceed that of the most reported EBFC using a BOD-based cathode (Fig. 5C).

Beyond output performance, we also conducted a comparative stability study of EBFCs catalyzed by Npyo-CDs, BOD, and BOD/Npyo-CDs. Under illumination, the open-circuit voltage of all three EBFCs remained stable for 70 h. However, during continuous discharge at 0.3 V for 70 h, the power output of EBFCs catalyzed by Npyo-CDs and BOD dropped significantly to 1 % and 52 % of their initial values, respectively. In contrast, the power output of EBFCs catalyzed by BOD/Npyo-CDs decreased by only 40 % (Fig. 5D). These findings strongly confirm two key aspects: First, BOD effectively mitigates the photo-corrosion of Npyo-CDs by promoting their photogenerated charges separation, thereby enhancing the stability of photoelectric conversion. Second, Npyo-CDs demonstrate without photo-toxicity toward BOD and further strengthen the interaction between BOD and the electrode, ultimately improving the stability of chemical-to-electrical energy conversion. Consequently, EBFCs catalyzed by BOD/Npyo-CDs exhibit exceptional electric energy conversion stability.

To address the problem that the BOD-catalyzed ORR performance is fast approaching its upper threshold, which restricts the further improvement of fuel cell's performance, we have designed a bio-abiotic hybrid ORR catalyst of BOD/Npyo-CDs with a triple synergistic enhancement effect. The Npyo-CDs, fabricated by carefully regulating the nitrogen doping species, efficiently convert solar energy into electrical energy. The LUMO energy level of the Npyo-CDs is lower than that of the BOD redox active center, allowing the additional photoelectrons to be utilized by BOD for enhanced ORR. This, in turn, promotes photoelectron-hole separation and mitigates photoelectron energy dissipation in Npyo-CDs. The HOMO energy level is sufficiently high to accept electrons from the EBFC anode. In addition, the ultra-small and highly conductive Npyo-CDs significantly shorten the electron transfer distance and accelerate the electron transfer rate. Benefiting from the triple synergistic enhancement effect, BOD/Npyo-CDs cathode exhibits a high current density of 2.76 mA cm−2, far suppressing that of BOD cathode and enabling the glucose/O2 fuel cell to deliver a remarkable power density (468 μW cm−2) and a high stable open-circuit voltage (0.78 V, 70 h). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first photo-bio-hybrid catalyst design featuring a triple synergistic enhancement effect, presenting a novel approach to overcoming the limitations in energy conversion efficiency.

Dandan Hu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Haoran Chen: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Qiwen Su: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Jian-Rong Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Linlin Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Jun-Jie Zhu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22374067, 22004060), the State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science (SKLACLS2408), the Sanqin Talent Project of Shaanxi Province (Linlin Wang), and the Central government special fund for guiding local science and technology development of Ningxia Hui Autonomous Region (2024FRD05098).
